text: The surgeon begins the procedure with lateral ports already placed, preparing for further surgical steps.
The surgeon completes the left-sided retro-rectus dissection and begins a straightforward crossover procedure in a patient with an untouched upper abdomen.
The surgeon extends the retro-rectus dissection well beyond the defect margins, ensuring broad mesh coverage over the hernia site to prevent recurrence.
The surgeon performs a standard dissection extending towards the patient's right semilunar line, focusing on the anatomical boundaries for mesh placement.
The surgeon works towards the peri-umbilical hernia, making an incision in the sheath.
The surgeon pulls down the hernia sac and peritoneum, separating them from the pseudosac in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a second hernia defect located three to five centimeters below the umbilicus.
The surgeon is now intraperitoneal, observing omentum that is incarcerated within the hernia defect.
The surgeon carefully dissects tissue while maintaining clear visualization to avoid injury to surrounding structures.
The surgeon exposes the neck of the hernia defect, providing a clear view of its anatomical location.
The video shows the incision site related to the prior C-section, providing a clear view of the surgical area.
The surgeon observes that much of the anterior sheath remains intact, visible within the surgical field.
The posterior layer of the sheath is visibly open, indicating it was never closed during the previous procedure.
The video shows limited amounts of tissue available for a preperitoneal repair, highlighting the constraints in the surgical field.
The surgeon assesses the surgical field, revealing multifocal defects, diastasis, and a larger hernia, suggesting a retromuscular approach for repair.
The surgeon performs placation of the midline, bringing the separated tissue edges together to reinforce the abdominal wall.
The surgeon incises the posterior sheath in the upper midline, where there is no diastasis, and proceeds to placate the fascia.
The surgeon ensures that the mesh is positioned to provide coverage extending to the upper portions of the dissected area, preparing for closure.
The surgeon uses a number one permanent V-lock suture to close the surgical site, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue layers.
The lower abdomen is visible, showing the placated area, as the surgeon addresses the lower defect.
The surgeon demonstrates ten centimeters of placation in the lower abdomen before reaching the defect, ensuring adequate mesh coverage.
The surgeon begins re-approximating the tissue layers using a number one permanent V-lock suture, following placation of the lower abdomen.
The surgeon exposes the peri-umbilical defect in the lower abdomen, which has been previously placated.
The surgical field shows the lower abdomen with visible placation, while the posterior aspect remains open and unclosed.
The surgeon completes the midline closure, suturing the visible tissue layers together along the midline.
The surgeon focuses on closing the posterior visceral sac, which remains open following the midline closure.
The surgeon measures the available space and confirms it can accommodate a 23 cm wide by 30 cm long macroporous polypropylene mesh.
The surgeon strategically rolls the macroporous polypropylene mesh to pass it smoothly through an eight millimeter trocar.
The surgeon unrolls the macroporous polypropylene mesh within the surgical field after inserting it through the trocar.
The surgeon ensures that the mesh is rolled completely to reach the ipsilateral semilunar line, maintaining proper alignment for placement.
The surgeon applies Tissio spray to fixate the mesh in place within the surgical field.
